text: A 2.5 cm fascial incision is made in the umbilicus to access the abdominal cavity, where the da Vinci single site robotic platform is introduced. The surgeon conducts an abdominal survey, identifying 2 cm proximal and 4-5 cm distal fallopian tubes bilaterally. Using monopolar electrosurgery at 20 watts of continuous power, areas of adhesions are resected, and occlusive devices such as fallope rings and filchi clips are mobilized from the fallopian tubes.

Dilute vasopressin is injected into the mesosalpinx, revealing significant anterior wall adiposity. The surgeon uses monopolar electrosurgery to free the fallopian tube segments from the underlying mesosalpinx, facilitating a tension-free suture line. Scissors are used to cut both ends of the adjoining fallopian tube, ensuring patency while preserving tissue length. Approximately five centimeters are removed from the obstructed ends to achieve patency. Chromoperturbation reveals blue dye, confirming patency in both proximal and distal segments. The lumens are identified, and tissue quality is confirmed with pink, well-perfused tissue at the ends of each fallopian tube segment.

The surgeon re-approximates the defect in the mesosalpinx with two stay sutures of 6-0 PDS at the junction between the mesosalpinx and the six o'clock portion of both fallopian tube edges. Tubal reanastomosis is performed using an 8-0 Surgipro suture, employing an outside-to-inside and inside-to-outside technique at cardinal headings to avoid knots in the lumen. A 6-0 Prolene suture reinforces the suture line along the fallopian tube serosa to prevent bowel adhesions. The procedure is repeated on the contralateral side, confirmed by chromopertubation showing fill and spill bilaterally.
